thread.title: Former Lackawanna County Prison internal investigator files whistleblower lawsuit
thread.title_full: Former Lackawanna County Prison internal investigator files whistleblower lawsuit

author: Jim Lockwood
title: Former Lackawanna County Prison internal investigator files whistleblower lawsuit
text:
SCRANTON — A former internal affairs investigator at the Lackawanna County Prison fired last year after only a few weeks on the job filed a whistleblower lawsuit, claiming he was terminated after reporting on “institutional corruption” at the jail.
The plaintiff, Joseph Harris, names as defendants Lackawanna County and the Lackawanna County Prison Board that oversees the lockup on North Washington Avenue. The lawsuit filed Oct. 16, 2024, in Lackawanna Court Court claims violation of the Pennsylvania Whistleblower Act because Harris was terminated after delivering a report detailing “fraud, abuse and waste” at the prison.
In a formal reply to the lawsuit filed in Lackawanna County Court, the defendants contend Harris was fired for missing work and his termination was not related to any whistleblowing by him.
The prison board voted to hire Harris on July 19, 2023, at a $70,000-a-year salary, to oversee internal affairs and investigate allegations of wrongdoing by prison guards, other staff and inmates. Harris started working for the county Aug. 2, 2023, but by Aug. 21, 2023, was no longer employed there. Then at its meeting in September of 2023, after meeting privately with Warden Tim Betti, the prison board voted “not to retain” Harris.
Plaintiff’s claims
The lawsuit’s other allegations include:
• Harris reported to Captain Eugene Ruddy and Deputy Warden David Pigga. In investigating the prison’s policies and procedures, Harris learned of “an active investigation into whether a county employee was illegally bringing narcotics into the prison, possibly for sale to prisoners, and if so, to what extent the employee’s superiors and prison administrators were aware of such illegal conduct,” and whether it was still taking place. Harris identified issues of “waste and/or wrongdoing,” but believed his investigative efforts were compromised by leaks and confidential sources’ fear of retaliation for their disclosures to him.
• By Aug. 14, 2023, Harris became convinced he could not proceed any further in his investigation and could not continue to do the job he was hired to do without intervention by the prison board. On Aug. 15, 2023, he prepared a written report noting his investigation had been compromised, the nature of it had been improperly leaked and/or disrupted by one or more individuals within the prison, and the chain of command that had him reporting to a deputy warden created a conflict of interest with his ability to investigate tactics of fear and intimidation. In the report, Harris requested an unpaid administrative leave pending a probe by the prison board of the findings in his report. He did not expect the leave of absence to last long, because the board was scheduled to meet the next day.
• Also on Aug. 15, Harris provided to county Commissioner Chris Chermak, who was a member of the prison board, seven sealed envelopes containing his report for delivery to the board members. “Chermak assured plaintiff that his request would be granted and that the plaintiff would not be terminated for reporting on wrongdoing,” the lawsuit claims.
• In an Aug. 16 phone call with Ruddy, Harris said he requested a leave of absence with the prison board. Harris also texted Chermak asking if all the members received the report. Chermak said he believed they did, except for Sheriff Mark McAndrew, who was on vacation.
• Harris received via mail a letter dated Aug. 22, 2023, and postmarked Aug. 23 telling him he was fired because he did not report to work from Aug. 16 through Aug. 21 and did not notify his supervisor of the absence. “Due to your unsuccessful performance during the introductory period of your employement, effective close of business August 21, 2023, your services will no longer be retained by the Lackawanna County Prison. This non-retention is contingent upon prison board approval,” the termination letter said.
• On Aug. 24, Harris ran into another member of the prison board, Lackawanna County District Attorney Mark Powell, who said he was not aware of Harris’s report. In an Aug. 25 text to Harris, Chermak said he gave the reports to the prison board chairman, Lackawanna County Judge James Gibbons. That same night, Harris spoke to prison board Solicitor David Solfanelli, who said he was called by Human Resources asking why Harris had been absent from work. Harris replied he had requested a leave of absence in the report. Solfanelli asked if Harris feared retaliation, and he said yes. Solfanelli told him he should make the leave request to Human Resources, and Harris did that the next day. Two days later, Harris got an email reply with termination letter.
• On Sept. 10, Harris emailed all prison board members in a group message saying he had provided a report for each member and was “attempting to report on what he believed to be institutional corruption” at the prison; and that he requested unpaid leave until he could meet with the board and requested that his employment not be terminated. On Sept. 20, upon the recommendation of Warden Betti, the board formally terminated Harris.
• The county violated his right to a “Loudermill hearing” to give him an opportunity to rebut the basis of the termination, the suit claims. It cites three counts of wrongful discharge and violations of the whistleblower law and due process.
Harris seeks over $50,000 in damages in the form of back pay, front pay, interest and compensatory and punitive damages. He is represented by attorney Michael Salmanson of the Salmanson Goldshaw law firm of Philadelphia.
Defendants’ rebuttal
In a formal objection to the lawsuit filed Nov. 18 by attorney Matthew Carmody of the Joyce, Carmody and Moran law firm of Pittston representing the county and board, the defendants seek dismissal of the complaint on several grounds.
Those include: Harris fails to state a claim for which relief can be granted under the whistleblower law because he does not specify waste or wrongdoing; or demonstrate there was specific direction given to him to not file his report, or there would be adverse consequences because the report was filed. The due-process claim also fails because Harris was an at-will employee subject to summary removal. And the wrongful termination count fails because, “even when an important public policy is involved, an employer may discharge an at-will employee if the employer has a separate, plausible and legitimate reason.” The county considered Harris’ absences unexcused as his leave was never approved, and he granted himself the leave unilaterally.
County Solicitor Don Frederickson also issued a statement Friday to The Times-Tribune saying: “The complaint filed by Mr. Harris is baseless and completely lacking in any meaningful legal claims. Mr. Harris was terminated from his position after failing to show up for work two weeks after he was hired, basically abandoning his position. As for his whistleblower claim, we are not sure what substantive information he could have discovered in less than two weeks on the job. The county fully intends to vigorously defend against this frivolous action.”
